title: Hospital Survey and Construction Act
summary: Authorized federal grants and loans for hospital construction and modernization through state plans, greatly expanding hospital infrastructure while permitting segregated implementation in many parts of the country.
outcome_summary: Expanded healthcare capacity nationwide but often reinforced segregated care and unequal access for Black patients until later civil rights enforcement and litigation challenged discriminatory administration.
impact_notes: A major public-health investment whose benefits were filtered through state and local systems that frequently upheld segregation.

scores.notes: Expanded physical access to care but left Black communities exposed to segregated and unequal hospital systems.

sources.notes: Federal program overview describing the 1946 law, its obligations, and its later compliance regime.

sources.source_title: Green Bill to End Hospital Discrimination Under the Hill-Burton Act
sources.publisher: Journal of the National Medical Association
sources.notes: Contemporary discussion of how Hill-Burton funding intersected with hospital discrimination.

title: Food Stamp Act of 1964
summary: Made the federal Food Stamp Program permanent, using nutrition assistance as a nationwide anti-poverty tool for households with low income.
outcome_summary: Expanded food assistance and became a major stabilizing support for low-income Black households, even though local administration and benefit adequacy remained uneven over time.
impact_notes: A durable anti-hunger program with substantial downstream effects on poverty, nutrition, and household stability.

scores.notes: Not race-specific, but highly consequential for communities facing concentrated poverty and food insecurity.

sources.notes: Statutory text for the law that made the Food Stamp Program permanent.

sources.source_title: The Many Reasons USDA is Celebrating 50 Years of SNAP
sources.notes: USDA overview of the program's anti-poverty role and its development from the 1964 law.

title: Social Security Amendments of 1965
summary: Created Medicare and Medicaid while expanding older-age and public assistance programs, making federal health coverage a permanent pillar of the welfare state.
outcome_summary: Greatly expanded health insurance access and became central to reducing racial gaps in medical coverage and care, especially through Medicaid and later expansions tied to it.
impact_notes: One of the most consequential federal health-policy expansions for Black Americans in the twentieth century.

scores.notes: Created the programs that remain foundational to health coverage for older adults, disabled people, and low-income households.

sources.notes: Congressional record for the law that created Medicare and expanded medical assistance under Medicaid.

sources.source_title: Loss of the Affordable Care Act Would Widen Racial Disparities in Health Coverage
sources.notes: Provides context on Medicaid's role in narrowing racial coverage gaps, which reflects the long legacy of the 1965 amendments.

title: Veterans' Readjustment Benefits Act of 1966
summary: Extended education, training, loan, and readjustment benefits to a new generation of veterans, including many who served during the Vietnam era.
outcome_summary: Expanded veteran benefits, but Black veterans still faced discrimination in housing, education, and local administration that limited equal access to the wealth-building potential of those supports.
impact_notes: Important expansion of veteran benefits whose practical returns remained constrained by broader racial inequality.

scores.notes: Helpful in design, but implemented within markets and institutions that still sharply limited Black veterans' returns.

sources.notes: Congressional record for the law extending readjustment assistance to veterans.

sources.source_title: Veterans' Readjustment Benefits Act of 1966 Actions
sources.notes: Action history confirming enactment and legislative progression.

title: Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act
summary: Expanded Medicaid, created insurance marketplaces, prohibited many coverage denials, and widened subsidized access to health insurance.
outcome_summary: Reduced uninsured rates and narrowed several racial disparities in health coverage and access, with especially important effects for Black adults in Medicaid expansion states.
impact_notes: A major health-equity expansion whose effects have depended partly on state implementation choices.

scores.notes: One of the most important modern health reforms for reducing uninsurance and improving access among Black Americans.

sources.source_title: Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act
sources.notes: Congressional record for the ACA and its enactment date.

sources.source_title: The Affordable Care Act and African Americans
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services
sources.notes: HHS overview of ACA provisions expected to improve coverage and healthcare access for African Americans.

sources.source_title: Loss of the Affordable Care Act Would Widen Racial Disparities in Health Coverage
sources.notes: Synthesizes research showing ACA and Medicaid expansion effects on racial coverage disparities.

sources.source_title: Health Insurance Coverage and Access to Care Among Black Americans: Recent Trends and Key Challenges
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services
sources.notes: HHS issue brief stating that since implementation of the ACA coverage provisions, the uninsured rate among nonelderly Black Americans decreased by 10 percentage points.

metrics.metric_name: Change in uninsured rate among nonelderly Black Americans after ACA coverage provisions
metrics.methodology_note: HHS reported in 2024 that since implementation of the Affordable Care Act coverage provisions, the uninsured rate among nonelderly Black Americans decreased by 10 percentage points.
